---
format:
  revealjs:
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-visibility: hidden 
    theme: [default, title_mc.scss]
    margin: 0.05
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    navigation-mode: vertical
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    embed-resources: true 
    self-contained: true
mainfont: "Cambria Math"
---

```{=html}
<section class="title-slide">

  <div class="header-band">
    <div class="logo-box">
      <img src="angrau_logo.jpeg" alt="ANGRAU Logo">
    </div>
    <div class="inst-block">
      <div class="university-name">
        Acharya N.G. Ranga Agricultural University
      </div>
      <div class="college-name">
        Agricultural College, Bapatla
      </div>
      <div class="dept-name">
        Department of Statistics and Computer Applications
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="mid-content">
    <div class="course-label">
      AECO 605 Operations Research (2+1)
    </div>
    <div class="main-title">
      Simulation - Random Numbers and Variables Generation, Monte Carlo Methods
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="footer-band">
    <div class="presenter-block">
      <span class="role-label">Presented by</span>
      <span class="person-name">Krupesh S</span>
      <span class="person-detail">(BAD/2025-25)</span>
      <span class="person-detail">I Ph.D.(Ag.) Agricultural Statistics</span>
    </div>
    <div class="incharge-block">
      <span class="role-label">Course In-charge</span>
      <span class="person-name">Dr. K. Suseela</span>
      <span class="person-detail">Professor and Head</span>
      <span class="person-detail">(Agricultural Economics)</span>
    </div>
  </div>

</section>
```

# INTRODUCTION

## Types of Simulation
```{=html}
<iframe src="system-flowchart.html"
        width="1280" height="720"
        style="border:none; display:block; margin:0 auto;"
        scrolling="no">
</iframe>
```

## Types of Simulation Models
```{=html}
<iframe src="simulation-types.html"
        width="1280" height="720"
        style="border:none; display:block; margin:0 auto;"
        scrolling="no">
</iframe>
```


## Steps in Sound Simulation Study
```{=html}
<iframe src="simulation_flowchart.html"
        width="1280" height="720"
        style="border:none; display:block; margin:0 auto;"
        scrolling="no">
</iframe>
```

# RANDOM NUMBER GENERATORS

## The Midsquare Method{style="text-align: justify;" .scrollable}
This is the earliest arithmetic random number generator, proposed by von Neumann and Metropolis in the 1940s. It produces a sequence of pseudo-random integers by repeatedly squaring a seed value and extracting the middle digits.

**Procedure:**

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}

**Input:** A four-digit seed integer $Z_0$

:::


1. **Square and pad:** Compute $Z_0^2$, padding with leading zeros to exactly **8 digits**

   $$Z_0^2 \;\xrightarrow{\text{pad}}\; \underbrace{d_1 d_2}_{}\underbrace{\boxed{d_3 d_4 d_5 d_6}}_{\text{middle 4}}\underbrace{d_7 d_8}_{}$$

2. **Extract:** Take the **middle 4 digits** $\Rightarrow Z_1$

3. **Normalise:** Set $\;U_1 = \dfrac{Z_1}{10000} \in (0,1)$

4. **Iterate:** For $i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$

   ::: {style="margin-left: 2em; border-left: 3px solid #4a9eff; padding-left: 1em;"}

   $$Z_i \;=\; \text{middle four digits of } Z_{i-1}^{\,2}$$

   $$U_i \;=\; \frac{Z_i}{10000}$$
  
   :::
   
## Linear Congruential Generator (LCG){style="text-align: justify;" .scrollable}   

Introduced by Lehmer (1951), LCGs are the most widely used class of pseudo-random number generators. They produce a sequence of integers via a simple linear recursion modulo a large integer $m$.

**Procedure:**

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}

**Input:** Seed $Z_0$, multiplier $a$, increment $c$, modulus $m$

:::

The recursive formula is:

::: {style="border-left: 3px solid #4a9eff; padding-left: 1em; margin: 0.5em 0;"}

$$Z_i = (a \cdot Z_{i-1} + c) \pmod{m}$$

:::

The $U(0,1)$ output is $U_i = Z_i / m \in [0,1)$.


## Period of a Generator{style="text-align: justify;" .scrollable}

The looping behavior of the LCG is inevitable. Whenever $Z_i$ takes on a value it has had previously, exactly the same
sequence of values is generated, and this cycle repeats itself endlessly.

::: {style="border-left: 5px solid #311B92; background-color: #f9f9fb; padding: 1em 1.5em; margin: 1.5em 0; border-radius: 0 6px 6px 0;"}


The length of such cycle is called period of a generator. For LCGs, the maximum attainable period is at most $m$. If the period of a generator is $m$, then the generator is said to have full period

:::

## Choosing LCG with full period{.scrollable}

::: {style="border-left: 5px solid #311B92; background-color: #f9f9fb; padding: 1em 1.5em; margin: 1.5em 0; border-radius: 0 6px 6px 0;"}

**Hull-Dobell Theorem (1962)**

The LCG has full period if and only if the following three conditions hold:

1. $\gcd(c, m) = 1$

2. $p \mid m \implies p \mid (a - 1) \quad \text{for all prime } p$


3. $4 \mid m \implies 4 \mid (a - 1)$

:::

Choose a higher value of $m \ge 10^9$

## Variants of the LCG {style="text-align: justify;" .scrollable}

The general **Linear Congruential Generator** recursion is:

::: {style="border-left: 3px solid #4a9eff; padding-left: 1em; margin: 0.5em 0;"}
$$Z_i = (a \cdot Z_{i-1} + c) \pmod{m}$$
:::

This is called the **Mixed Congruential Generator** — it uses both a multiplier $a$ and a non-zero increment $c$.

By restricting the parameters, two important special cases arise:

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Multiplicative Congruential Generator
Set $c = 0$:
$$Z_i = a \cdot Z_{i-1} \pmod{m}$$

- Simpler and faster; widely used in practice
- Cannot achieve full period (since $Z_i = 0$ is absorbing when $c = 0$)
- Seed $Z_0 \neq 0$ is required

:::

::: {.callout-warning icon=false}
## Additive Congruential Generator  
Set $a = 1$:
$$Z_i = (Z_{i-1} + c) \pmod{m}$$

- Equivalent to repeatedly adding a constant increment $c$
- Poor statistical properties; rarely used alone

:::
